old_claim.note: Blood Typing provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Clinical blood banking as scoped includes donor-recipient typing and matching, so ABO grouping and crossmatching are required safety methods.

source_shape.source_locator: AABB timeline: 1900 Landsteiner discovers the first three human blood groups; 1907 Hektoen suggests crossmatching and Ottenberg performs transfusion using blood typing and crossmatching.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: AABB places ABO blood-group discovery and blood typing plus crossmatching before the institutional blood-bank entries.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Because the node includes controlled transfusion, typing, and matching, the compatibility methods are part of the blood-bank workflow rather than optional background knowledge.

ontology_before: Clinical blood banking treated blood typing as a generic enabling capability.
ontology_after: Clinical blood banking requires blood typing and crossmatching as the direct compatibility method for safe stored-blood transfusion.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: clinical_blood_banking -> blood_typing is now a required edge.
would_reject_if: The node is later narrowed to blood collection/storage only and excludes matching or transfusion safety.
A stronger source shows early hospital blood banks did not use blood typing or crossmatching.
The blood_typing node is rescoped away from ABO grouping and transfusion compatibility.
short_reason: AABB identifies blood typing and crossmatching as transfusion safety methods before hospital blood banks.
